data.title: Corn Futures Drop as Quarter Nears End — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais 




  --Corn for December delivery fell 0.5% to $4.24 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade Wednesday, with traders getting their books in order ahead of the end of the third quarter next week. 



  --Soybeans for November delivery fell 0.3% to $10.08 1/2 a bushel. 



  --Wheat for December delivery fell 0.2% to $5.19 1/2 a bushel. 





  HIGHLIGHTS 





  Final Touches: Grain traders are shaping their positions for the end of the third quarter on Sept. 30, and also getting ahead of a quarterly stocks report from the USDA that could put new weight on futures. "We now have but four sessions to the end of the quarter, which is always a big accounting deadline for the funds," says Charlie Sernatinger of Marex in a note. Sernatinger adds that while pressure may be seen in the coming days, short-covering could be the avenue that grain futures, particularly corn, sees higher prices. 



  Alternative Sources: More soybeans from Argentina are going to China after Argentina's government cut export taxes on grains. "China had bought 10-12 cargoes of soybeans," said John Stewart & Associates in a note. "That number has increased to nearly 20 cargoes, equating to roughly 1.3 million metric tons of soybeans." It signals China is opting to choose South American alternatives rather than buy U.S. soybean exports, continuing a hands-off approach as trade negotiations between China and the U.S. continue. 





  INSIGHT 





  Stubborn Schism: President Trump's overture to discuss his 50% tariffs on Brazil with Brazilian President Lula may not lead to a sustained dialogue. The relationship "is more likely to remain strained," Eurasia Group analysts said in a note. Trump has linked the tariffs to what he sees as unfair prosecution of his local ally, former President Bolsonaro - who was found guilty by the country's Supreme Court of plotting against the elected government. "Lula cannot alter Bolsonaro's conviction " and trade concessions are also unlikely, Eurasia said. A Trump-Lula connection, however, "could at least limit the potential for the rift to escalate." 



  Line in the Sand: If the situation surrounding U.S. export tariffs escalates, then the world agricultural market-and global markets at large-may find themselves bifurcated by two groups of powers, said Gerrit Marx, CEO of agricultural equipment supplier CNH. Marx said that CNH is preparing for a situation where tariffs and retaliatory measures essentially split the world in two - one side being nations aligned under BRICS, and the other being the 'western world,' or countries organized under the G7 banner. "We work with scenarios," Marx told the WSJ in an interview. "There's a scenario where we go back to reasonable tariffs-in that particular world, there is no distinction between a BRICS-led world and the western world." CNH sells agricultural equipment around the world under several different brands. 



  High Volume: The USDA has again reported new high-volume sales of U.S. agricultural exports this week, with Mexico again purchasing a large amount of U.S. corn. The USDA says that 312,956 metric tons of corn were sold to Mexico for delivery in the 2025/26 marketing year, along with 101,400 tons of soybean meal sold to Guatemala for delivery in 2025/26. Mexico has been a frequent buyer of large lots of U.S. corn over the past week. The increased pace of buying comes as CBOT corn futures climb back from a multi-year low found in mid-August. 





  AHEAD 





  --The USDA will release its weekly Export Sales report at 8:30 a.m. ET Thursday. 



  --The USDA will release its quarterly Hogs and Pigs report at 3 p.m. ET Thursday. 



  --The USDA will release its monthly Cold Storage report at 3 p.m. ET Friday. 



  --The CFTC will release its weekly Commitment of Traders report at 3:30 p.m. ET Friday. 





  Paulo Trevisani contributed to this article. 



  Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 24, 2025 15:31 ET (19:31 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: ICE Review: Canola Slightly Higher, Underpinned by Lack of Farmer Selling
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Intercontinental Exchange canola futures clung to small increases as a lack of farmer selling continued to underpin prices.

Canola received additional support Wednesday from gains in Malaysian palm oil and most MATIF rapeseed contracts. Pressure from declines in the Chicago soy complex eroded canola's stronger upticks earlier in the session.

An upswing in crude-oil prices spilled over into the vegetable oils.

The Prairie weather forecast calls for good harvest conditions, which should increase the pace. Manitoba reported its overall harvest was 56% finished with the province's canola at 45% done.

The Canadian dollar dropped to 71.92 U.S. cents compared to Tuesday's close of 72.30.

There were 35,666 contracts traded on Wednesday, compared to 54,712 on Tuesday. Spreading accounted for 19,888 contracts traded. 




  Prices are in Canadian dollars per metric ton:

data.title: AM Best Assigns Issue Credit Rating to American Financial Group, Inc.’s New Senior Unsecured Notes
data.description:
AM Best Assigns Issue Credit Rating to American Financial Group, Inc.’s New Senior Unsecured Notes

AM Best has assigned a Long-Term Issue Credit Rating of “a-” (Excellent) to the recently issued $350 million, 5% 10-year senior unsecured notes due September 2035, issued by American Financial Group, Inc. (AFG) (Cincinnati, OH) [NYSE: AFG]. The outlook assigned to this Credit Rating (rating) is stable. The existing ratings of AFG and its subsidiaries are unchanged.

AFG plans to use the net proceeds of the offering for general corporate purposes including share repurchase. Following the new issuance, AFG’s unadjusted financial leverage ratio will increase modestly to nearly 30% and remain within AM Best’s guidelines for AFG’s current ratings. AFG’s interest coverage ratio will remain strong at over 10 times.

AFG continues to benefit from consistently positive operating earnings of its insurance subsidiaries, which are reflective of profitable underwriting results supported by a diversified product portfolio and business profile through its multiple distribution platforms, in addition to significant investment income.

This press release relates to Credit Ratings that have been published on AM Best’s website. For all rating information relating to the release and pertinent disclosures, including details of the office responsible for issuing each of the individual ratings referenced in this release, please see AM Best’s Recent Rating Activity web page. For additional information regarding the use and limitations of Credit Rating opinions, please view Guide to Best's Credit Ratings. For information on the proper use of Best’s Credit Ratings, Best’s Performance Assessments, Best’s Preliminary Credit Assessments and AM Best press releases, please view Guide to Proper Use of Best’s Ratings & Assessments.

AM Best is a global credit rating agency, news publisher and data analytics provider specializing in the insurance industry. Headquartered in the United States, the company does business in over 100 countries with regional offices in London, Amsterdam, Dubai, Hong Kong, Singapore and Mexico City. For more information, visit www.ambest.com.

data.title: This government program could be a boon for Gen Z and millennials trying to save for retirement
data.description:
By Alicia H. Munnell

Here's how it could be improved

The Saver's Match could have a meaningful impact on retirement wealth at 65 for Generation Z and millennial workers.

I have always been a great fan of providing a government match on the retirement savings for low- and middle-income workers.

These workers, who pay very little in income taxes, gain virtually nothing from the tax preferences accorded retirement saving and can afford to put aside only modest amounts. Indeed, the inclusion of a new Saver's Match was one of the few redeeming features of Secure 2.0.

So, I'm delighted that a recent study from Morningstar provides some estimates about the possible impact of the Saver's Match on retirement wealth. Nevertheless, I remain uneasy about how this new provision is actually going to work.

Under the new program, beginning in 2027, the government will provide eligible taxpayers a 50% match on the first $2,000 of contributions to a retirement plan. Eligibility is based on household income. Single taxpayers with $20,500 or less qualify for the full match, and the match phases out at $35,500. For couples, the comparable numbers are $41,000 for the full match, with the match phased out at $71,000.

The Saver's Match replaces the Saver's Credit program, which has generally been viewed as a failure - less than 6% of taxpayers claimed the credit in 2021. The major problem was that the credit was nonrefundable, which means that it could only be used to reduce the taxpayer's income-tax liability. And the interaction with the Child Tax Credit meant that the Saver's Credit was often not fully usable for taxpayers with children. On top of those limitations, most workers were simply unaware of the program.

The new Saver's Match attempts to address these problems. Most important, the match is refundable. That is, the legislation changes it from a credit used to reduce a liability to a government matching contribution deposited into the taxpayer's retirement plan. In addition, the legislation includes some funds for Treasury to increase public awareness about the availability of the program.

As one would expect, and the estimates from the Morningstar study confirm, the Saver's Match could have a meaningful impact on retirement wealth at 65 for Generation Z and millennial workers - those who will spend a major part of their work lives under the new program. The analysis is done under four behavioral scenarios:

-- No behavioral changes: The match is only used by low-to-moderate income savers who are already predicted to save in a retirement plan.

-- New savers: Individuals who do not save under the baseline will start saving due to the incentive created by the Saver's Match. The responses are based on a 2024 survey that asked respondents - not already saving - the likelihood that they would start saving.

-- New savers and active savers save more: In addition to assuming that new savers enter (scenario 2), this scenario assumes that active savers increase their saving to get the full match. Again, the likelihood is based on the 2024 survey.

-- Higher probability of new Savers and active savers saving more: This scenario increases the probabilities assumed in scenario 3.

The results show that, depending on the behavioral response, the Saver's Match could increase retirement wealth at 65 for Gen Z and Millennials by 8% to 12% (see Figure 1).

Despite these encouraging results, I continue to worry about the mechanics. How is the government going to know where to send the match, and how will it move funds mechanically? How is the Saver's Match - which is a pretax contribution - going to mesh with Roth accounts, which are for posttax contributions? Since Roths are widely used in state auto-IRA programs, the states will need to ensure that workers have access to a separate traditional account for receiving the Saver's Match.

So far, they appear to be stepping up, but the withdrawal process may still be hard for workers to understand. For example, how do you explain to people that they can withdraw their own contributions to a Roth tax-free immediately, the earnings on those contributions tax-free after five years if they are age 591/2, and their taxable Saver's Match contributions at 591/2?

So, while it's nice to have some idea about the impact of the Saver's Match, I would feel much better if I understood how this thing is actually going to work.

Like every successful program, the Saver's Match needs to be easy and automatic. The amounts are modest, so participants should not be penalized for a mistake. Someone smarter than me can figure out a clear way to explain the withdrawal options.

The Saver's Match is a wonderful step forward, let's make it work.

-Alicia H. Munnell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-24-25 1520ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 3 PM ET: New-home sales spike to 3-year high as builders pile on discounts to sell homes  | U.S. ...

New-home sales spike to 3-year high as builders pile on discounts to sell homes

Sales of new homes posted an unexpected jump in August as builders ramped up discounts and slashed prices to lure buyers.

----

U.S. Stocks Lower As Tech Continues to Recede

Stocks were modestly lower as the tech sector pulled back for a second day.

----

China to Stop Seeking Special WTO Treatment in Future Talks

Chinese Premier Li Qiang framed the decision as coming from "a responsible major developing country," underscoring that Beijing continues to claim the status of a developing nation.

----

U.S. Crude Oil Inventories Post Weekly Decline

U.S. crude oil inventories fell by 607,000 barrels last week along with declines in product stocks , according to data released Wednesday by the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

----

Bessent Says U.S. Talking With Argentina About $20 Billion in Aid

The South American nation faces a market run on its peso amid questions about its economic prospects.

----

German Companies Turn Gloomier as Economy Flounders

Confidence fell in September for the first time this year as companies said they were less satisfied with current conditions and saw a deterioration in manufacturing and services.

----

Canada Factory Sales Estimated to Have Fallen 1.5% in August

Factory activity in Canada looks to have stalled, with an early estimate of sales showing a pullback in August following a recovery the previous two months.

----

New Zealand Appoints First Female Central Bank Governor

The New Zealand government has appointed Anna Breman from Sweden's central bank as the country's next central bank governor.

----

Australian Inflation Accelerates to Highest Level in a Year

Inflation pressures are once again building in Australia, with a monthly reading hitting its highest level in just over a year in August.

----

Wall Street Is Poaching Bankers in a Red-Hot Job Market

Dealmaking is picking up, and big firms are hunting for more bankers, even as hiring slows in many industries.

----

Get Rich or Get Wiped Out: Bitcoin's Hottest New Trade

Perpetual futures offer traders extreme leverage to bet on cryptocurrencies.

----

Stephen Miran's Rate-Cut Arguments Don't Add Up

The Trump policy changes that Miran cites as justification for lower rates don't necessarily deliver as promised, or have side effects.

----

Bank of Canada's Macklem Warns U.S. Dollar Losing Appeal as Hedge

Foreign investors are hedging more of their U.S.-dollar exposure, thereby applying downward pressure on the dollar, the Canadian central banker said.

  September 24, 2025 15:15 ET (19:15 GMT)

data.title: Employer-sponsored emergency-savings accounts -2-
data.description:
Savings advocates say employee demand for these programs is there, too. In a 2023 Commonwealth report, 72% of polled workers said they would participate in a hypothetical emergency-savings program offered by their employer, especially if they were offered a reward for consistent savings.

Meanwhile, Secure 2.0 has opened up simple options to employers looking to stay within their retirement plan.

The $2,500 account-balance limit the law allows is less than the cost of many emergencies, and far less than the six- to 12-month fund experts recommend. Still, research from Vanguard released this year found that having "$2,000 in emergency savings is the strongest predictor of financial well-being" and can significantly reduce financial stress and improve focus and workplace productivity.

It is, in other words, much better than nothing.

If you're just starting out on your money or career journey and have questions about how to navigate your finances, we want to hear from you. Write to Dollar Signs, MarketWatch's new advice column, at dollarsigns@marketwatch.com.

-Venessa Wong

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: Employer-sponsored emergency-savings accounts are becoming a hot perk. Here's how they work.
data.description:
By Venessa Wong

Americans, squeezed by high prices and a weak job market, struggle to save for emergencies on their own. In response, many companies are offering emergency savings as a workplace benefit.

In one survey, 79% of employers said emergency savings had become an "extremely important" or "very important" focus within their financial well-being programs.

As an employer, Noreen King said, one of her goals is "to make sure everyone's OK" - including when emergencies come up for her workers.

While her medical-device company, Evolve Manufacturing, had given workers financial assistance on an ad hoc basis over the past 25 years, it didn't offer such aid to employees across the board. So when the CEO heard about SecureSave - a fintech platform that allows employers to open and contribute to worker-owned emergency-savings accounts - she saw it as a more systemic way to help employees prepare for the unexpected.

Since Evolve introduced this savings benefit in October 2021, about 75% of employees have opted in, she said. Those who enroll receive a $25 contribution from Evolve per paycheck, and can also choose to automatically contribute a dollar amount from their after-tax pay.

"It really cost me very, very little, but it's a great benefit," King told MarketWatch.

One Evolve employee, who asked not to be named, said when her mother got sick in 2024, she was able to pay for a plane ticket home using her workplace emergency-savings account rather than having to borrow the money from a relative. In total, she has withdrawn $8,000. Another worker said he used $200 from his account to replace a tire that blew out on his way to work.

As savings have become the leading financial priority for Americans amid stubbornly high prices and a weaker labor market - and as employers look to boost productivity by improving workers' financial well-being - a growing number of employers are introducing emergency-savings accounts, or ESAs, as a dedicated benefit alongside established workplace savings programs such as 401(k)s and health savings accounts.

Related: Americans are 'revenge saving' after years of splurging: 'Savings are a great way to have some certainty'

Meanwhile, a Joe Biden-era law called the Secure 2.0 Act has the potential to further proliferate mini savings funds in the next few years, experts told MarketWatch. Starting in 2024, this law allowed employers to auto-enroll workers in so-called in-plan ESAs, meaning those linked to existing retirement accounts, up to a maximum balance of $2,500. It made emergency savings a policy priority.

When "you've got income insufficiency challenges and rising costs - particularly healthcare, transportation, housing, the things you just need to live - and you put all of these things together, I think it's why emergency-savings accounts are having a moment," Matt Bahl, vice president of workplace at the nonprofit Financial Health Network, told MarketWatch.

Already, 37% of employers say they offer some form of emergency savings, according to a 2024 survey by the Employee Benefit Research Institute - although the most common form was still a 401(k) loan, which financial experts say should be a last resort, as it threatens future retirement funds.

Dedicated ESAs, a relatively new benefit category, are drawing plenty of attention: 79% of employers in the EBRI survey said emergency savings had become an "extremely important" or "very important" focus within their financial-wellbeing programs.

Fidelity, which started offering an "out-of-plan" ESA called Goal Booster in 2019, told MarketWatch the company "has seen demand for emergency-savings benefits from employers nearly double each year over the past two years - a clear sign of momentum." Out-of-plan ESAs are those not linked to a retirement plan.

Vanguard, which launched its Cash Plus Account as an out-of-plan emergency-savings option for employers in 2024, told MarketWatch it has "seen strong adoption and growth."

Meanwhile, SecureSave enrollment is up about 20% over the last year. Another out-of-plan ESA platform, Sunny Day Fund, told MarketWatch its number of enrolled employers doubled this year.

How workplace emergency-savings accounts work

While financial-wellness experts recommend automating emergency savings from the paycheck, as is standard for retirement, only 25% of Americans do so, according to a 2025 NerdWallet poll. Instead, people tend to save at random times when they have extra money. The result: 32% of people say they have no emergency savings, and the median amount saved for emergencies is $500, according to an Empower survey in June.

The next stop, for many, is high-interest credit-card debt. One in four people surveyed by Bankrate in May said they would pay for an unexpected emergency by financing it with a credit card and paying it off over time.

Related: 5 mental tricks to reset your relationship with credit cards as Americans pile on debt

About six years ago, employers started testing different kinds of emergency-savings benefits to help address the issue.

"When you have access to an [account] connected to your paycheck that's essentially vetted by your employer, and a continued message that this is something you can do, it really can help people to get closer to" their savings goals, said Timothy Flacke, cofounder and CEO of Commonwealth, a nonprofit focused on improving financial stability for low- and moderate-income workers.

Employers have several options: Outside of retirement plans, they can partner with platforms such as SecureSave and Sunny Day Fund to enable workers to set aside part of their paycheck into regular FDIC-insured savings accounts at partner banks - similar to a split direct deposit - and can offer a contribution or match. Both of these services charge employers a fee based on the size of their workforce. For example, SecureSave charges small businesses $99 per month and large enterprises $999 per month.

Companies can also add out-of-plan ESAs through their existing benefits providers; for instance, Fidelity, Vanguard and T. Rowe Price offer workplace clients an out-of-plan emergency-savings product at no charge.

Starting last year, changes under the Secure 2.0 Act created two additional options through workplace retirement plans. First, they allowed employers to automatically enroll employees who are not highly compensated - in 2025, the limit is $151,164 - in dedicated in-plan emergency-savings accounts and contribute up to 3% of their paychecks, up to a balance of $2,500.

The hope is that the automatic-enrollment feature within retirement plans, which is not permitted in out-of-plan accounts, "could supercharge [the ESAs'] effect on savers' overall financial security," according to a report from the Bipartisan Policy Center.

Second, Secure 2.0 gave employers the option to allow penalty-free withdrawals from retirement accounts of up to $1,000 in a year for emergencies, with no further withdrawals for the next three years unless the distribution was repaid.

Although workers with out-of-plan accounts have full access to their dollars at any time, SecureSave embeds behavioral-finance psychology in the product design "to make sure people aren't endlessly raiding the accounts," Devin Miller, SecureSave co-founder and CEO, told MarketWatch.

For instance, the banks don't issue debit cards for these accounts. Also, while SecureSave doesn't define what an emergency is or limit what the money can be used for, the app prompts users to specify the emergency for which they need the funds. The buttons also use labels like "Get Emergency Money" instead of "Withdraw" or "Transfer."

These features "create a little bit of a speed bump," Miller said. "If you just call this a generic 'savings account,' people start to disassociate it from emergency savings" and end up using it for routine consumption.

So far, the top five reasons SecureSave users said they needed emergency funds were inflation (16%), car/transportation (7%), home (6%), healthcare (5%) and travel (4%).

How emergency savings became a workplace issue

The effort to build emergency savings as a workplace-benefit category kicked off in earnest in 2019, when the investment firm BlackRock launched a $50 million program called the Emergency Savings Initiative with Commonwealth, the Financial Health Network and Duke University's Common Cents Lab to develop employer-based savings solutions for low- to moderate-income workers.

Sunny Day Fund founder Sid Pailla launched his platform that year as well. "The default [in the U.S.] is getting a paycheck and saving for retirement - that's it," Pailla told MarketWatch. "You're not getting automated into saving up for everything in life that happens between now and retirement. You're supposed to figure that out on your own."

Soon after, SecureSave, co-founded by Suze Orman, launched in 2020 as high unemployment rates during the pandemic exposed the financial precarity of the average American worker.

"It was a watershed moment; building emergency savings into the fabric of an organization was no longer a question of if, but how," a BlackRock report said.

Research later showed increased productivity and improved worker safety resulted from employees feeling less financially stressed, solidifying a business case for workplace emergency savings.

Is America on the verge of a workplace-savings revolution?

Heightened economic volatility this year has spurred some personal-finance experts to suggest keeping six to 12 months' worth of expenses in emergency savings. And with Americans naming saving as their top financial goal, interest in workplace savings programs is high, surveys show.

Read more: Your cash emergency fund probably isn't going to cut it in this economy

In addition to the 37% of employers that told EBRI in 2024 they already offered an emergency-savings fund, another 40% said they planned to offer such a benefit in the next year or two.
